front: Opera: the soprano aria 'La mamma morta' is sung by Maddalena to Gerard, who retracts his accusations against her poet lover; Love tells her to keep living after her house was burned and mother killed
Opera: ends with the title Revolutionary poet and his lover Maddalena executed by guillotine during the Reign of Terror

Opera: the aria 'Un dì all'azzurro spazio' is nicknamed 'L'Improvviso' because it follows the claim that true poetry cannot be produced on command; the title character sings of nature vs. the suffering of the poor
Opera: opens with an apostrophe to a blue sofa as a servant's son watches his father adjust its cushions
Opera: the spy l'Incrédibile sees through the maid Bersi's disguise at the Café Hottot in Act II while following the title character
Opera: baritone Gerard makes false accusations against the title character, then retracts them, but the Revolutionary Tribunal still imprisons and executes him
Opera: Cyrano-esque scene in which Maddalena admits to sending anonymous love letters to the title character; they pledge their love in 'Ora soave'
Opera: Gerard's baritone aria 'Nemico della Patria' has him question his own motives as a revolutionary
Opera: libretto by Luigi Illica, who also wrote libretti for Puccini's La Bohème, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly (with Giacosa)
Opera: 'La mamma morta' contains the lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter'; Maria Callas's recording was used in the film Philadelphia
Opera: celebrity sopranos including Elena Obraztsova, Rosalind Plowright, and Fedora Barbieri have made cameo appearances playing old blind Madelon
Opera: tenor Enrico Caruso launched to fame in the premiere as Loris Ipanov, a nihilist who sings 'Amor ti vieta' and is accused of murdering Vladimir
Opera: based on a Victorien Sardou play; Sardou refused Giordano twice before allowing the adaptation; centers on Princess Fedora accusing the nihilist Loris Ipanov
Opera: the title character reminds Napoleon he owes her sixty francs for laundry
Composer: key figure of Italian verismo opera, alongside Mascagni and Leoncavallo; best known for an opera about a French Revolutionary poet
